html:
Inkeverse Thesis

Inkeverse is a cheap, messy, ignored Hong Kong small cap where the market is pricing two things at once: a dying Chinese live-streaming app and a management team that will never let shareholders touch the cash. That discount is understandable, but it now looks too punitive.

The 2025 numbers were not clean growth, but they were materially better than the stock price implies. Revenue fell 25.2% to RMB5.13 billion, MAUs fell 10.8%, and ARPU fell 4.4%, yet operating profit rose 87.3%, adjusted net profit rose 24.5% to RMB292 million, and profit attributable to owners rose 33.8% to RMB241 million in the 2025 annual results. This is not a top-line momentum story yet. It is a cost discipline, mix shift, and asset-value story with real optionality.

At roughly HKD0.91 per share, market cap is about HKD1.76 billion. That is around 5.2x 2025 adjusted earnings and 6.3x owner earnings after translating RMB profit into HKD. More important, the balance sheet is absurd relative to the equity value: cash, term deposits, and financial assets at fair value were roughly RMB3.36 billion at year-end, against only RMB143 million of borrowings. Even after haircutting restricted cash, listed-equity volatility, and the post-year-end HKD300 million GoFintech subscription, net financial resources are still plausibly around HKD1.70 to HKD1.90 per share before assigning value to the operating business.

That does not make it a slam dunk. Idle cash alone is not upside torque. Inkeverse paid no final dividend, disclosed no company share buybacks in 2025, and has been pushing capital into treasury products, Web3.0, AI, and financial assets. The May 2026 Da Cheng money-market filing is a useful window into the problem: the underlying product was low risk, but the company admitted it mishandled listing-rule disclosure and had to set remedial controls. The cash discount exists for a reason.

The variant view is that the market is over-discounting that governance mess and underpricing the business transition. Inkeverse is no longer just an old live-streaming monetization vehicle chasing scale. Management is deliberately shrinking lower-quality revenue, leaning into live social networking, content services, AIGC-enabled playlets and comic dramas, overseas operations in Southeast Asia and MENA, and AI-assisted matching and production workflows. The annual report language is promotional, but the financial evidence is real: gross margin improved to 50.0%, sales and marketing was cut sharply, headcount fell to 1,334, and the company stayed profitable while revenue reset lower.

The biggest analytical mistake would be to treat the 2025 reported profit as purely recurring. A large piece of operating profit came from RMB343 million of other gains, mainly fair-value gains on financial assets. Core operating profit before those gains is much thinner. That is why this should not be valued like a clean 5x earnings compounder. The better framing is: a surviving social/content platform with RMB100 million to RMB250 million of normalized core earnings power is attached to liquid assets worth far more than the entire market cap.

The forward setup is still attractive. If 2026 revenue stabilizes around RMB4.7 billion to RMB5.1 billion and management holds gross margin near 49% to 51%, the business can earn RMB220 million to RMB300 million even without another huge investment gain. That puts the stock at roughly 5x to 7x forward earnings, while the operating business is effectively valued at less than zero after net financial assets. If overseas social products or AIGC content even modestly work, the earnings base can move back toward RMB350 million to RMB450 million.

There are catalysts. The May 6 rally, when the stock traded up sharply on heavy volume after investors digested the 2025 results and the AI/Web3/overseas narrative, showed that retail attention can return quickly. The April 24 custody transfer equal to 15.07% of shares into Morgan Stanley is not proof of a new strategic holder, but it does show that a large block is in motion. Any confirmation in the 2026 interim that cash is intact, revenue decline is slowing, and the company is not destroying capital in side investments could matter. A dividend, buyback, tender offer, or privatization would matter much more.

The bear case is straightforward: users keep leaving, ARPU keeps falling, investment gains reverse, management burns the balance sheet on speculative AI/Web3 stakes, and minority shareholders never see the cash. The local regulatory investigation tied to RMB28.5 million of restricted cash is small financially but another reminder that this is not a clean governance story.

Still, the asymmetry is hard to ignore. The stock does not need a heroic growth outcome. It needs the company to remain profitable, stop leaking credibility, and let the market believe that even half the balance sheet belongs to shareholders.

Bear target: HKD0.55, down 40%. This assumes the operating business is valued near zero, investment gains reverse, and the market applies a severe discount to cash and financial assets.

Base target: HKD1.80, up 98%. This assumes RMB240 million to RMB300 million of forward normalized earnings, a 6x to 7x multiple on the operating business, and only partial recognition of net financial assets.

Bull target: HKD3.20, up 252%. This assumes revenue stabilizes, overseas/content/AI initiatives produce renewed growth, normalized earnings move above RMB400 million, and management unlocks value through a buyback, dividend, tender, or privatization.
